name: Glycerol, 99%+ (anhydrous)
striking_fact: 1400× more viscous than water — standard pipetting speed causes air bubble entrapment and large volume errors.

sds_facts.storage: Store at room temperature, tightly sealed to prevent moisture uptake.

bench_knowledge: Use reverse pipetting: press plunger to second stop before aspirating, then release to first stop. This eliminates the dead air volume that causes bubbles.
After aspirating, wait 2–3 seconds before withdrawing the tip from the liquid — glycerol clings to the outer tip and drips after removal.
Wide-bore tips cut aspiration resistance and reduce shear; standard tips build enough back-pressure to stall standard pipette pistons at high volumes.
Glycerol is hygroscopic — an open bottle gains mass (and dilutes concentration) as it absorbs atmospheric water. Cap immediately after use.
Freezing point is 17.8°C: pure glycerol can solidify in a 4°C cold room or on ice. Warm to room temperature and confirm it is fully liquid before pipetting.
Glycerol-containing buffers (50% enzyme stocks, glycerol stocks of bacteria) stay liquid at −20°C; exploited for bacterial cryopreservation without ice crystal damage.
